title: The French Revolution
summary: A decade of radical political and social upheaval that overthrew the French monarchy, ended feudal privileges, and reshaped ideas of citizenship and nationhood across Europe.
background: By the late 1780s, France faced a severe financial crisis, an unequal tax system that spared the nobility and clergy, and widespread public frustration with royal absolutism under Louis XVI.
causes: Chronic royal debt worsened by costly wars, including support for the American Revolution
A rigid social hierarchy that concentrated privilege among the nobility and clergy
Widespread food shortages and rising bread prices in the 1780s
The spread of Enlightenment ideas questioning absolute monarchy

keyPeople.name: Napoleon Bonaparte
keyPeople.role: Rose to power at the Revolution's end

keyPeople.name: Maximilien Robespierre
keyPeople.role: Leading figure of the Reign of Terror

timeline.label: Storming of the Bastille; National Assembly formed
France adopts its first written constitution
Execution of Louis XVI; Reign of Terror begins
Fall of Robespierre ends the Terror
Napoleon's coup ends the revolutionary government

mapNote: Centered on Paris and spreading through French provinces, with revolutionary and counter-revolutionary conflict extending to France's borders and colonies.
consequences: Abolition of feudal privileges and the old estate system
Execution of the king and establishment of a republic
Rise of Napoleon Bonaparte and the Napoleonic Wars
Spread of revolutionary and nationalist ideas across Europe
longTermImpact: The Revolution permanently weakened the idea of divine-right monarchy in Europe and popularized concepts of citizenship, nationalism, and secular government that shaped 19th- and 20th-century politics.

title: World War II
summary: The deadliest conflict in human history, fought between the Axis and Allied powers across Europe, Asia, Africa, and the Pacific, ending with the defeat of Nazi Germany and Imperial Japan.
background: Rising fascist and militarist regimes in Germany, Italy, and Japan pursued territorial expansion through the 1930s, straining the postwar order established after World War I and the League of Nations' ability to contain aggression.
causes: Punitive terms and instability following World War I
The rise of Nazi Germany under Adolf Hitler and expansionist policy
Japanese expansion in East Asia and the Pacific
Failure of appeasement and collective security to deter aggression

keyPeople.name: Winston Churchill
keyPeople.role: Prime Minister of the United Kingdom

keyPeople.name: Adolf Hitler
keyPeople.role: Chancellor and Führer of Nazi Germany

timeline.label: Germany invades Poland; Britain and France declare war
Fall of France; Battle of Britain
Germany invades the USSR; Japan attacks Pearl Harbor
Allied D-Day landings in Normandy
Germany surrenders in May; Japan surrenders in September after atomic bombings

mapNote: Fought across European, North African, and Pacific theaters, involving nearly every major power and dozens of countries worldwide.
consequences: Estimated tens of millions of military and civilian deaths, including the Holocaust
Formation of the United Nations in 1945
Beginning of the Cold War between the United States and Soviet Union
Decolonization movements accelerated across Asia and Africa
longTermImpact: The war reshaped the global balance of power, established the U.S. and USSR as competing superpowers, and led to new international institutions aimed at preventing future large-scale conflict.

title: The Indian Independence Movement
summary: A decades-long political struggle that ended nearly 200 years of British colonial rule in India, culminating in independence and partition in 1947.
background: British colonial administration controlled India through the British Raj following the dissolution of the East India Company's rule in 1858, governing a diverse subcontinent while facing growing demands for self-rule.
causes: Economic exploitation and restrictive colonial trade policy
Growth of Indian nationalist organizations, notably the Indian National Congress (founded 1885)
Mass mobilization campaigns led by Mahatma Gandhi and others
Weakening of British global power following World War II

keyPeople.name: Jawaharlal Nehru
keyPeople.role: First Prime Minister of independent India

timeline.label: Indian National Congress founded
Gandhi leads the Salt March
Quit India Movement launched
India and Pakistan gain independence amid partition

mapNote: Spanned the Indian subcontinent, culminating in the 1947 partition into India and Pakistan and large-scale population movement across the new border.
consequences: End of British colonial rule in South Asia
Partition of British India into India and Pakistan
Large-scale displacement and communal violence during partition
Establishment of India as the world's largest democracy
longTermImpact: The movement became a model for nonviolent resistance worldwide and reshaped the geopolitical map of South Asia, while partition's legacy continues to influence India-Pakistan relations.

title: The Fall of the Berlin Wall
summary: The 1989 collapse of the barrier dividing East and West Berlin, marking the symbolic end of the Cold War division of Europe.
background: Since 1961, the Berlin Wall had physically divided the city, separating communist East Berlin from West Berlin and standing as the most visible symbol of the Iron Curtain dividing Europe.
causes: Growing economic stagnation across the Eastern Bloc
Reform movements under Soviet leader Mikhail Gorbachev, including glasnost and perestroika
Mass pro-democracy demonstrations across East Germany in 1989
A miscommunicated East German announcement easing travel restrictions

keyPeople.name: Mikhail Gorbachev
keyPeople.role: Soviet leader whose reforms enabled political change

keyPeople.name: Egon Krenz
keyPeople.role: East German leader during the wall's fall

timeline.label: Berlin Wall constructed, dividing the city
East German officials announce eased travel rules
Crowds breach the wall at checkpoints
Formal reunification of Germany

mapNote: Centered on the city of Berlin, divided between the Federal Republic of Germany (West) and the German Democratic Republic (East).
consequences: Rapid collapse of communist governments across Eastern Europe
German reunification in October 1990
Acceleration of the broader dissolution of the Soviet Union by 1991
longTermImpact: The fall of the wall is widely seen as the defining symbolic moment of the Cold War's end, reshaping the political map of Europe and accelerating European integration.

title: The Apollo 11 Moon Landing
summary: NASA's Apollo 11 mission achieved the first crewed Moon landing on July 20, 1969, fulfilling a national goal set by President Kennedy earlier in the decade.
background: The mission came amid the Cold War-era Space Race between the United States and the Soviet Union, following the Soviet Union's early lead with satellite and human spaceflight milestones in the late 1950s and early 1960s.
causes: Cold War competition for technological and ideological prestige
President John F. Kennedy's 1961 commitment to a crewed lunar landing by decade's end
Sustained investment in the U.S. space program through NASA

keyPeople.name: Neil Armstrong
keyPeople.role: Commander; first person to walk on the Moon

keyPeople.name: Buzz Aldrin
keyPeople.role: Lunar module pilot; second person on the Moon

keyPeople.name: Michael Collins
keyPeople.role: Command module pilot, remained in lunar orbit

timeline.label: Kennedy announces the goal of a crewed Moon landing
Apollo 11 launches from Kennedy Space Center
Lunar module Eagle lands; Armstrong steps onto the Moon
Crew safely returns to Earth

mapNote: Launched from Cape Kennedy, Florida, with the lunar landing occurring in the Sea of Tranquility on the Moon.
consequences: United States achieves the first crewed Moon landing, effectively ending the lunar phase of the Space Race
Major advances in aerospace, computing, and materials technology
Renewed global public interest in space exploration
longTermImpact: Apollo 11 remains a defining symbol of 20th-century technological achievement and laid groundwork for subsequent space exploration programs.

title: The American Civil War
summary: A four-year war between the Union and the secessionist Confederacy, fought primarily over slavery and states' rights, ending in Union victory and the abolition of slavery.
background: Deep sectional divisions over the future of slavery, especially in new territories, had grown for decades, intensifying after the 1860 election of Abraham Lincoln, whose party opposed slavery's expansion.
causes: Long-standing disputes over the expansion of slavery into new territories
Southern states' secession following Lincoln's 1860 election
Diverging economic systems between the industrial North and agrarian, slaveholding South
Disputes over federal versus states' rights

timeline.label: Lincoln elected president; South Carolina secedes
War begins at Fort Sumter
Emancipation Proclamation; Battle of Gettysburg
Confederate surrender at Appomattox; Lincoln assassinated

mapNote: Fought primarily across the Southern and border states of the United States, with major campaigns in Virginia, Tennessee, and the Mississippi Valley.
consequences: Abolition of slavery via the Thirteenth Amendment
Preservation of the United States as a single nation
Beginning of the Reconstruction era
Hundreds of thousands of deaths, the deadliest conflict in U.S. history
longTermImpact: The war's outcome ended slavery in the United States and set in motion long, contested struggles over civil rights and federal authority that continued for generations.
